examples.title: Simple Shell Command Processing
examples.description: Demonstrates how a shell interprets and executes a user command
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ExecuteCommand(command)
  BEGIN
    parsedCommand <- PARSE(command)
    IF parsedCommand IS valid THEN
      argumentList <- EXTRACT_ARGUMENTS(parsedCommand)
      executablePath <- FIND_EXECUTABLE(parsedCommand.commandName)
      IF executablePath EXISTS THEN
        CREATE_NEW_PROCESS()
        LOAD_PROGRAM(executablePath, argumentList)
        EXECUTE_PROCESS()
        RETURN "Command executed successfully"
      ELSE
        RETURN "Command not found"
      ENDIF
    ELSE
      RETURN "Invalid command syntax"
    ENDIF
  END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the basic steps a shell takes: parsing the input command, finding the executable, creating a process, and executing it. The shell acts as an intermediary between user and OS kernel.

examples.title: System Call Interface Mechanism
examples.description: Demonstrates how system calls transition from user mode to kernel mode
examples.code:
// User Space
FUNCTION UserReadFile(fileDescriptor, buffer, size)
  BEGIN
    // Prepare parameters for system call
    syscallNumber <- READ_SYSCALL_NUMBER
    params <- [fileDescriptor, buffer, size]
    
    // Trigger transition to Kernel Mode
    result <- TRIGGER_SYSCALL(syscallNumber, params)
    
    RETURN result
  END

// Kernel Space (OS handles this)
FUNCTION KernelReadFileHandler(syscallNumber, params)
  BEGIN
    VERIFY_USER_PERMISSIONS(currentProcess, params.fileDescriptor)
    
    IF permissionGranted THEN
      data <- READ_FROM_DISK(params.fileDescriptor, params.buffer, params.size)
      RETURN data
    ELSE
      RETURN ERROR_PERMISSION_DENIED
    ENDIF
  END
examples.explanation: This shows how a system call works: user program calls library function which triggers a special instruction (like syscall in x86) that switches the CPU from user mode to kernel mode. The kernel performs the privileged operation (file reading) and returns control to user space.

examples.title: Simple CLI Command Simulation
examples.description: Python program simulating basic shell command interpretation
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates a basic command-line shell. It shows the shell loop, command parsing, and execution. Real shells do much more including I/O redirection, piping, environment variables, and scripting capabilities.

examples.title: System Call Example in C
examples.description: Demonstrates how system calls work using open() and read() functions
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates three important system calls: open(), read(), and close(). Each of these triggers a transition from user mode to kernel mode. The open() system call requests the OS to open a file, read() reads data from it, and close() releases the file descriptor. The return value -1 indicates an error occurred.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - proportional to file size read
